chunk_summary: This section discusses fasting lipid interpretation in pediatric insulin-resistance dyslipidemia, emphasizing triglycerides, HDL-C, and non-HDL-C cutpoints for metabolic risk management using NHLBI guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the lipid profile patterns associated with pediatric insulin-resistance dyslipidemia.
Learn NHLBI cutpoints for triglycerides, HDL-C, and non-HDL-C in children and adolescents.
Identify secondary causes of dyslipidemia relevant to Indian practice before diagnosing primary dyslipidemia.
Differentiate between fasting and nonfasting lipid testing strategies for universal screening and metabolic risk clinics.
key_facts: Insulin-resistance dyslipidemia is characterized by high triglycerides, low HDL-C, and elevated non-HDL-C.
NHLBI triglyceride cutpoints: ages 0–9 acceptable <75 mg/dL, borderline 75–99, high ≥100; ages 10–19 acceptable <90, borderline 90–129, high ≥130.
NHLBI HDL-C cutpoints: acceptable >45 mg/dL, borderline 40–45, low <40.
Non-HDL-C cutpoints: acceptable <120 mg/dL, borderline 120–144, high ≥145.
Non-HDL-C testing is useful in nonfasting states and captures all atherogenic particles.
reference_quote: Use the NHLBI pediatric cutpoints for interpretation: Triglycerides ages 0–9 acceptable <75 mg/dL, borderline 75–99, high ≥100; ages 10–19 acceptable <90, borderline 90–129, high ≥130.
document_key_topics: NHLBI lipid cutpoints for pediatric dyslipidemia
Fasting versus nonfasting lipid testing strategies
Secondary causes of dyslipidemia in Indian practice
